Okay, welcome back. In this lesson we are going to explore another technique for unpacking the situational gestalt. This is called compassionate challenging. So what is compassionate challenging? Well, it all comes back down to the ethos we already spoke about, which is to not validate, confirm, or over sympathize. Sympathize? Yes, sympathize with limiting beliefs or false conclusions. So you will want to use this compassionate challenging when you sense through your presencing to them that they've locked themselves into some sort of a false conclusion or a belief that is laced with victimhood and disempowerment. So how might this appear? Right? It usually comes in the tonality of their voice and some kind of statement that just feels a little dense, a little heavy, a little defeatist, right? So it might sound something like, well, I guess I just have to grind this out. I guess this is happening because I'm just kind of built differently. I'm just different than those other people. I suppose my relationship is tougher than others and I just have to kind of roll and live with that, right? So any kind of form of this disempowering false conclusion, this defeatist mentality is something that you're going to want to compassionately challenge, right? It's not about being adversarial, but at the same time, if you allow that statement, right, of something defeatist to just kind of live, right, and go unchallenged, you're essentially allowing the call to take on this tonality of them remaining disempowered, right? And this once again, robs them of that meta-state of feeling powerful. So that's why we do it. So to intervene with compassionate, challenging, you still want to remain neutral and free of judgment and simply ask a very light, a very easy, a very simple version of, is that entirely true? So that is an example of a challenging statement. Once again, not being adversarial, not being judgmental, just simply asking with curiosity, is that entirely true? So you can acknowledge, I acknowledge that your path has been challenging, but is it absolutely true that it has to remain that way? Or just because that's how it's been, does that mean for sure that that's how it will always be? Is that entirely true? Or just been true up to now in your personal experience? Have you really been stuck? Or is it possible that you're on the same trajectory of all those other successful people you look up to were on once upon a time? So these are questions that don't have a leading answer, right? You're not trying to get them to, you're not trying to force them to see a silver lining, you're not trying to force them into reframing themselves into a degree of empowerment, you're just trying to create a simple crack where they can challenge or feel like they could challenge their own beliefs that they may have solidified. And these are beliefs that once again, are of massive disservice, right, to their ability to feel empowered to approach new opportunity. So the challenge is meant to do nothing more than expose a crack within the concreteness that they've been orienting around. And I'm always brought back to our friend Leonard Cohen, right? There's a crack in everything. That's how the light gets in, right? So you could think of these rigid, hardened, concretized beliefs and this defeatist mentality as this slab of concrete, right? But if you could just help them have this little crack in it, right? That is where the light gets in and exposes the rest and gives them opportunity to see and view and experience things, perhaps in a different way. And it's from that chasm that new insight, possibility and resourcefulness can be born. So that is compassionate, challenging. You will want to use this whenever through your presencing, you feel a new sense and you perceive that they have some sort of concretized, defeatist, limiting belief around a topic that they need to explore a little bit more deeply and reconsider, re-explore. Is there something here that I'm perhaps not seeing? So that's it. Use this wisely once again, use this skillfully, and I will meet you in the next lesson where we will explore another coaching technique. I'll see you there.
